Here are the complete results for all four queries:

---

## RESULT 1: Papers NOT containing rs9923231 or VKORC1

The following 10 papers (out of 41 total) contain neither "rs9923231" nor "VKORC1":

```
/app/papers/PMC2014334.md
/app/papers/PMC2745926.md
/app/papers/PMC2976715.md
/app/papers/PMC2993460.md
/app/papers/PMC3505921.md
/app/papers/PMC3691683.md
/app/papers/PMC3778124.md
/app/papers/PMC4567270.md
/app/papers/PMC5464525.md
/app/papers/PMC6385304.md
```

---

## RESULT 2: Titles and Abstracts of the 10 Non-rs9923231 Papers

### PMC2014334
**Title:** Frequency of cytochrome P450 CYP2C9 variants in a Turkish population and functional relevance for phenytoin
**Authors:** Aynacioglu et al., British Journal of Clinical Pharmacology, 1999
**Abstract:** Studies CYP2C9*2 and *3 allele frequencies in 499 Turkish subjects and correlates genotype with phenytoin pharmacokinetics. No mention of VKORC1 or rs9923231. The anticoagulant mention is only incidental (EDTA blood collection tubes). The only warfarin mention is in references about CYP2C9's role in S-warfarin metabolism. The paper's focus is entirely on phenytoin as a CYP2C9 probe drug.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC2745926
**Title:** Nitric oxide synthase (NOS3) variants and disease-free survival among treated and untreated breast cancer patients in a SWOG Clinical Trial (S8897)
**Authors:** Choi et al., Clinical Cancer Research, 2009
**Abstract:** Examines relationships between disease-free survival and variants in NOS3, NQO1, NQO2 and CBR3 in breast cancer patients enrolled in a Southwest Oncology Group clinical trial. Finds a significant interaction between NOS3 Glu298Asp and -786 polymorphisms and adjuvant chemotherapy outcomes. Entirely focused on breast cancer and oxidative stress genes.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC2976715
**Title:** Role of Organic Cation Transporter 3 (SLC22A3) and Its Missense Variants in the Pharmacologic Action of Metformin
**Authors:** Chen et al., Pharmacogenetics and Genomics, 2010
**Abstract:** Determines that OCT3 plays a role in metformin's pharmacologic action (AMPK activation) in skeletal muscle. Identifies and functionally characterizes 6 novel missense variants of OCT3. Topic is metformin and organic cation transport, with no connection to warfarin or anticoagulation.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC2993460
**Title:** Ecto-5'-Nucleotidase and Thiopurine Cellular Circulation: Association with Cytotoxicity
**Authors:** Li et al., Drug Metabolism and Disposition, 2010
**Abstract:** Studies the thiopurine pathway (6-MP and 6-TG) in 194 lymphoblastoid cell lines. Identifies a "thiopurine cellular circulation" involving NT5E, ABCC4, and nucleoside transporters. Entirely focused on leukemia chemotherapy pharmacogenomics.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC3505921
**Title:** Influence of Methylenetetrahydrofolate Reductase C677T, A1298C, and G80A Polymorphisms on the Survival of Pediatric Patients with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
**Authors:** Deus et al., Leukemia Research and Treatment, 2012
**Abstract:** Analyzes MTHFR and RFC1 polymorphisms and overall survival in 126 pediatric ALL patients treated with methotrexate. The C677T TT genotype shows better survival (~98%) while 677CC shows lower survival. Entirely in the ALL/methotrexate/folate metabolism space.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC3691683
**Title:** Initial Evidence of an Association between OPRM1 and Adolescent Alcohol Misuse
**Authors:** Miranda et al., Alcoholism: Clinical and Experimental Research, 2009
**Abstract:** Examines the OPRM1 A118G SNP (rs1799971) and alcohol use disorder in 187 adolescents. Finds that 51.9% of youth with AUD carry at least one G allele vs. 16.3% of controls. Topic is opioid receptor genetics and adolescent alcohol misuse.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC3778124
**Title:** Variation in the Alpha 5 Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Subunit Gene Predicts Cigarette Smoking Intensity as a Function of Nicotine Content
**Authors:** MacQueen et al., The Pharmacogenomics Journal, 2013
**Abstract:** Investigates CHRNA5 polymorphisms and smoking topography in 66 smokers. Finds that genotype at rs16969968 predicts nicotine titration (puff volume). Topic is nicotine addiction and CHRNA5 genetics.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC4567270
**Title:** Cholelithiasis and Nephrolithiasis in HIV-Positive Patients in the Era of Combination Antiretroviral Therapy
**Authors:** Lin et al., PLoS ONE, 2015
**Abstract:** Retrospective review of 910 HIV-positive patients examining prevalence and risk factors for gallstones and kidney stones. Ritonavir-boosted atazanavir for >2 years is associated with cholelithiasis. Examines UGT1A1*28 and MDR1 G2677T/A polymorphisms (not VKORC1). Topic is HIV/antiretroviral pharmacogenomics.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC5464525
**Title:** Genetic variants in ALDH1B1 and alcohol dependence risk in a British and Irish population
**Authors:** Way et al., PLoS ONE, 2017
**Abstract:** Uses bioinformatic techniques to characterize ALDH1B1 missense variants (rs2073478, rs2228093, rs4878199, rs142427338) and genotypes 1478 alcohol-dependent cases vs. 1254 controls. No significant allelic associations found. Topic is alcohol metabolism genetics (ALDH/ADH) and alcohol dependence risk.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC6385304
**Title:** Polymorphism A118G of opioid receptor mu 1 (OPRM1) is associated with emergence of suicidal ideation at antidepressant onset
**Authors:** Nobile et al., Scientific Reports, 2019
**Abstract:** Studies rs1799971 (OPRM1) and rs105660 (OPRK1) in 3566 depressed outpatients receiving tianeptine. Finds AA genotype of rs1799971 associated with treatment-emergent suicidal ideation (OR=1.93). Topic is opioidergic system, depression, and suicidality.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

## RESULT 3: Drug Names Beyond Warfarin in VKORC1/rs9923231-Positive Papers

There are 31 papers mentioning VKORC1/rs9923231. Here are the substantive (non-reference) matches for non-warfarin VKA drugs and coumarin-class terms:

### Papers with substantive body-text mentions:

**PMC3384479** — "Vitamin K antagonists in children with heart disease..." — THE richest source:
- **Fluindione** mentioned extensively as the actual drug used in 35 of 118 children studied
  - "warfarin (n = 83) or fluindione (n = 35) maintenance dose"
  - Mean weekly fluindione dose: 123.1 ± 52.2 mg (range 45–250 mg); 4.07 ± 2.68 mg/kg
  - "The VKORC1 genotype was the only variable significantly associated with the weekly fluindione maintenance dose (P = .0363)"
  - "The effect of CYP2C9 on non-coumarin VKAs such as fluindione is unclear"
  - "Two patients receiving acenocoumarol were not analyzed" (acenocoumarol excluded)
  - Section heading: "Influence of genetic and nongenetic variables on maintenance dose in fluindione-treated patients"
  - VKORC1 rs9923231 directly associated with fluindione dose

**PMC3175513:**
- "The coumarins are the most used class of oral anticoagulants, to which warfarin belongs"
- "SNPs...affect the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of coumarins, respectively"

**PMC2911553:**
- "Warfarin, a coumarin derivative, exerts its anticoagulant effect by inhibiting vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1)"

**PMC3125052:**
- "Warfarin/Acitrom and related coumarins are widely used in thromboembolic prophylaxis"
- Acitrom (acenocoumarol equivalent used in India) is the second drug studied alongside warfarin in this paper

**PMC4209173:**
- "Warfarin, a synthetic coumarin used for anticoagulation"

**PMC5859345:**
- Keywords include: "vitamin K antagonist, VKORC1, warfarin"

**PMC6714673:**
- "Warfarin (coumarin) therapy is one of the many clinical situations..."
- "prevent the action of warfarin (vitamin K antagonist)"

### References-only mentions (cited drugs, not studied in the paper itself):
- **Acenocoumarol**: cited in PMC2515139, PMC2586955, PMC3038469, PMC4296935, PMC5006145 (all reference citations, e.g., Schalekamp et al. on VKORC1/CYP2C9 and acenocoumarol; Bodin et al. on acenocoumarol sensitivity)
- **Phenprocoumon**: cited in PMC2586955 (Schalekamp et al. on VKORC1/CYP2C9 and phenprocoumon), PMC4296935 (Luxembourg et al. on phenprocoumon therapy)
- **Coumarin (general)**: referenced in PMC3718044, PMC3753327, PMC4702374, PMC4706412, PMC5508045, PMC6489578

**Summary of drugs found:**
| Drug | Status in papers |
|---|---|
| Warfarin | Primary drug studied across most papers |
| Fluindione | Actually studied in PMC3384479 (35 patients); VKORC1 significantly associated with dose |
| Acenocoumarol | Studied in PMC3125052 (as "Acitrom"); cited in references of 5+ papers |
| Phenprocoumon | Referenced only (cited papers) in PMC2586955, PMC4296935 |
| Coumarin (class) | Referenced descriptively in multiple papers |

---

## RESULT 4: Phenotype/Dosage-Related Terms in VKORC1/rs9923231-Positive Papers

Key substantive matches across papers (filtered for body text, not reference URLs):

**PMC2515139:**
- "Both VKORC1 and CYP2C9 polymorphisms independently correlate with warfarin dose and other clinical outcomes such as **time to stabilized dose, bleeding events, and time within the target therapeutic range**"
- "Combined polymorphisms...explain approximately 30% of the variance in the stabilized warfarin dose distribution"

**PMC2911553:**
- "the most commonly used oral anticoagulant for patients with **mechanical heart valve replacement** (MHVR) to prevent **thromboembolism**"
- "the narrow therapeutic index and the wide inter-individual variation...to minimize the adverse events and to shorten the time to reach the target INR"

**PMC3125052:**
- "The subtherapeutic and supratherapeutic use of these drugs is associated with an increased risk of clot formation and **bleeding**"
- "the dose requirements of these anticoagulants vary between individuals"
- "interindividual variations in the VKORC1-1639 G>A gene polymorphism has been linked to differences in the **dose requirements** for oral anticoagulants"
- "patients taking Acitrom after **valve replacement surgery**"

**PMC3175513:**
- "SNPs in CYP2C9 and VKORC1 genes...are strongly associated with warfarin dose"
- "rs9923231...associated with a reduction of approximately 30% in warfarin dose"
- "The dose to achieve the target INR ranges from an average of 4 mg to 80 mg weekly"

**PMC3384479:**
- "VKAs have a narrow **therapeutic range** and considerable inter- and intra-individual **dose-response variability**"
- "height, target INR and VKORC1 and CYP2C9 genotypes were the main determinants of **warfarin dose requirement**, accounting for 48.1%, 4.4%, 18.2%, and 2.0% of variability"
- "prevent **thromboembolism** after **cardiac valve replacement**, total cavopulmonary connection, dilated cardiomyopathy, coronary aneurysms after Kawasaki disease"
- "patients had **mitral valve replacement** with a target INR of 3.3"
- "rs9923231 located in the functional promoter of VKORC1 require substantially lower doses"

**PMC4209173:**
- "Indications for paediatric use include treatment of **thromboembolic disorders**, prophylaxis for **heart valve replacement**"
- "**therapeutic monitoring** through the use of the International Normalised Ratio"
- "wide inter-individual variation in the maintenance dose required (0.5 mg/day to 20 mg/day)"

**PMC6714673:**
- "VKORC1 and CYP2C9 polymorphisms are the major predicting factors of **warfarin dose requirement**"
- "vitamin K–rich diet can prevent the action of warfarin...and may need high warfarin maintenance dose"

**Key phenotype categories found across all 31 VKORC1-positive papers:**
| Phenotype | Evidence |
|---|---|
| Warfarin/VKA dose requirement | Core phenotype in virtually all papers; quantitative dose (mg/week) |
| Dose variability | Consistently described; VKORC1+CYP2C9 explain 30-55% of variance |
| Therapeutic range / INR control | Time in therapeutic range (TTR) mentioned; INR targets specified |
| Bleeding risk | Referenced as key adverse outcome of over-anticoagulation |
| Thromboembolism | Referenced as indication for therapy and outcome of under-anticoagulation |
| Heart valve replacement | Frequent indication (mechanical valve, mitral/aortic valve) |
| Atrial fibrillation | Appears as an indication in several papers |
| Time to stable dose | Mentioned as clinical outcome in PMC2515139 |
| Over-anticoagulation | Referenced in citations (Schalekamp acenocoumarol paper title) |

---

**Summary:**

1. All 10 papers listed in Result 1 are confirmed NOT relevant to rs9923231 — they cover CYP2C9/phenytoin pharmacogenetics (PMC2014334), breast cancer survival (PMC2745926), metformin/OCT3 transport (PMC2976715), thiopurine/leukemia (PMC2993460), MTHFR/ALL survival (PMC3505921), OPRM1/alcohol in adolescents (PMC3691683), CHRNA5/nicotine (PMC3778124), HIV/atazanavir stones (PMC4567270), ALDH1B1/alcohol dependence (PMC5464525), and OPRM1/suicidal ideation (PMC6385304).

2. The 31 VKORC1-positive papers cover warfarin and VKA dosing pharmacogenetics. Beyond warfarin, the key non-warfarin drugs mentioned in substantive body text are: **fluindione** (actually studied in PMC3384479, with VKORC1 directly linked to its dose), **acenocoumarol/Acitrom** (studied in PMC3125052; cited in references elsewhere), and **phenprocoumon** (referenced in citations only). The primary phenotypes documented are warfarin/VKA dose requirement, INR control/therapeutic range, bleeding, thromboembolism, and cardiac indications (heart valve replacement, atrial fibrillation)."}

**Title:** Frequency of cytochrome P450 CYP2C9 variants in a Turkish population and functional relevance for phenytoin
**Authors:** Aynacioglu et al., British Journal of Clinical Pharmacology, 1999
**Abstract:** Studies CYP2C9*2 and *3 allele frequencies in 499 Turkish subjects and correlates genotype with phenytoin pharmacokinetics. No mention of VKORC1 or rs9923231. The anticoagulant mention is only incidental (EDTA blood collection tubes). The only warfarin mention is in references about CYP2C9's role in S-warfarin metabolism. The paper's focus is entirely on phenytoin as a CYP2C9 probe drug.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC2745926
**Title:** Nitric oxide synthase (NOS3) variants and disease-free survival among treated and untreated breast cancer patients in a SWOG Clinical Trial (S8897)
**Authors:** Choi et al., Clinical Cancer Research, 2009
**Abstract:** Examines relationships between disease-free survival and variants in NOS3, NQO1, NQO2 and CBR3 in breast cancer patients enrolled in a Southwest Oncology Group clinical trial. Finds a significant interaction between NOS3 Glu298Asp and -786 polymorphisms and adjuvant chemotherapy outcomes. Entirely focused on breast cancer and oxidative stress genes.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

**Title:** Role of Organic Cation Transporter 3 (SLC22A3) and Its Missense Variants in the Pharmacologic Action of Metformin
**Authors:** Chen et al., Pharmacogenetics and Genomics, 2010
**Abstract:** Determines that OCT3 plays a role in metformin's pharmacologic action (AMPK activation) in skeletal muscle. Identifies and functionally characterizes 6 novel missense variants of OCT3. Topic is metformin and organic cation transport, with no connection to warfarin or anticoagulation.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

**Title:** Ecto-5'-Nucleotidase and Thiopurine Cellular Circulation: Association with Cytotoxicity

**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

**Authors:** Deus et al., Leukemia Research and Treatment, 2012
**Abstract:** Analyzes MTHFR and RFC1 polymorphisms and overall survival in 126 pediatric ALL patients treated with methotrexate. The C677T TT genotype shows better survival (~98%) while 677CC shows lower survival. Entirely in the ALL/methotrexate/folate metabolism space.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC2976715
**Title:** Role of Organic Cation Transporter 3 (SLC22A3) and Its Missense Variants in the Pharmacologic Action of Metformin
**Authors:** Chen et al., Pharmacogenetics and Genomics, 2010
**Abstract:** Determines that OCT3 plays a role in metformin's pharmacologic action (AMPK activation) in skeletal muscle. Identifies and functionally characterizes 6 novel missense variants of OCT3. Topic is metformin and organic cation transport, with no connection to warfarin or anticoagulation.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

### PMC2993460
**Title:** Ecto-5'-Nucleotidase and Thiopurine Cellular Circulation: Association with Cytotoxicity
**Authors:** Li et al., Drug Metabolism and Disposition, 2010
**Abstract:** Studies the thiopurine pathway (6-MP and 6-TG) in 194 lymphoblastoid cell lines. Identifies a "thiopurine cellular circulation" involving NT5E, ABCC4, and nucleoside transporters. Entirely focused on leukemia chemotherapy pharmacogenomics.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

### PMC3505921
**Title:** Influence of Methylenetetrahydrofolate Reductase C677T, A1298C, and G80A Polymorphisms on the Survival of Pediatric Patients with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
**Authors:** Deus et al., Leukemia Research and Treatment, 2012
**Abstract:** Analyzes MTHFR and RFC1 polymorphisms and overall survival in 126 pediatric ALL patients treated with methotrexate. The C677T TT genotype shows better survival (~98%) while 677CC shows lower survival. Entirely in the ALL/methotrexate/folate metabolism space.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

**Title:** Initial Evidence of an Association between OPRM1 and Adolescent Alcohol Misuse
**Authors:** Miranda et al., Alcoholism: Clinical and Experimental Research, 2009
**Abstract:** Examines the OPRM1 A118G SNP (rs1799971) and alcohol use disorder in 187 adolescents. Finds that 51.9% of youth with AUD carry at least one G allele vs. 16.3% of controls. Topic is opioid receptor genetics and adolescent alcohol misuse.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

### PMC3778124
**Title:** Variation in the Alpha 5 Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Subunit Gene Predicts Cigarette Smoking Intensity as a Function of Nicotine Content
**Authors:** MacQueen et al., The Pharmacogenomics Journal, 2013
**Abstract:** Investigates CHRNA5 polymorphisms and smoking topography in 66 smokers. Finds that genotype at rs16969968 predicts nicotine titration (puff volume). Topic is nicotine addiction and CHRNA5 genetics.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

### PMC4567270
**Title:** Cholelithiasis and Nephrolithiasis in HIV-Positive Patients in the Era of Combination Antiretroviral Therapy
**Authors:** Lin et al., PLoS ONE, 2015
**Abstract:** Retrospective review of 910 HIV-positive patients examining prevalence and risk factors for gallstones and kidney stones. Ritonavir-boosted atazanavir for >2 years is associated with cholelithiasis. Examines UGT1A1*28 and MDR1 G2677T/A polymorphisms (not VKORC1). Topic is HIV/antiretroviral pharmacogenomics.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

**Title:** Genetic variants in ALDH1B1 and alcohol dependence risk in a British and Irish population
**Authors:** Way et al., PLoS ONE, 2017
**Abstract:** Uses bioinformatic techniques to characterize ALDH1B1 missense variants (rs2073478, rs2228093, rs4878199, rs142427338) and genotypes 1478 alcohol-dependent cases vs. 1254 controls. No significant allelic associations found. Topic is alcohol metabolism genetics (ALDH/ADH) and alcohol dependence risk.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

### PMC6385304
**Title:** Polymorphism A118G of opioid receptor mu 1 (OPRM1) is associated with emergence of suicidal ideation at antidepressant onset
**Authors:** Nobile et al., Scientific Reports, 2019
**Abstract:** Studies rs1799971 (OPRM1) and rs105660 (OPRK1) in 3566 depressed outpatients receiving tianeptine. Finds AA genotype of rs1799971 associated with treatment-emergent suicidal ideation (OR=1.93). Topic is opioidergic system, depression, and suicidality.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

## RESULT 3: Drug Names Beyond Warfarin in VKORC1/rs9923231-Positive Papers

There are 31 papers mentioning VKORC1/rs9923231. Here are the substantive (non-reference) matches for non-warfarin VKA drugs and coumarin-class terms:

### Papers with substantive body-text mentions:

**PMC3384479** — "Vitamin K antagonists in children with heart disease..." — THE richest source:

  - "The effect of CYP2C9 on non-coumarin VKAs such as fluindione is unclear"

**PMC3175513:**

**PMC2911553:**

**PMC3125052:**

- Acitrom (acenocoumarol equivalent used in India) is the second drug studied alongside warfarin in this paper

**PMC4209173:**

**PMC5859345:**

**PMC6714673:**

- "prevent the action of warfarin (vitamin K antagonist)"

### References-only mentions (cited drugs, not studied in the paper itself):
- **Acenocoumarol**: cited in PMC2515139, PMC2586955, PMC3038469, PMC4296935, PMC5006145 (all reference citations, e.g., Schalekamp et al. on VKORC1/CYP2C9 and acenocoumarol; Bodin et al. on acenocoumarol sensitivity)

**Summary of drugs found:**
| Drug | Status in papers |
|---|---|
| Warfarin | Primary drug studied across most papers |
| Fluindione | Actually studied in PMC3384479 (35 patients); VKORC1 significantly associated with dose |
| Acenocoumarol | Studied in PMC3125052 (as "Acitrom"); cited in references of 5+ papers |
| Phenprocoumon | Referenced only (cited papers) in PMC2586955, PMC4296935 |

## RESULT 4: Phenotype/Dosage-Related Terms in VKORC1/rs9923231-Positive Papers

Key substantive matches across papers (filtered for body text, not reference URLs):

**PMC2515139:**
- "Both VKORC1 and CYP2C9 polymorphisms independently correlate with warfarin dose and other clinical outcomes such as **time to stabilized dose, bleeding events, and time within the target therapeutic range**"

**PMC2911553:**

**PMC3175513:**
- "SNPs in CYP2C9 and VKORC1 genes...are strongly associated with warfarin dose"

**PMC3384479:**
- "VKAs have a narrow **therapeutic range** and considerable inter- and intra-individual **dose-response variability**"

- "prevent **thromboembolism** after **cardiac valve replacement**, total cavopulmonary connection, dilated cardiomyopathy, coronary aneurysms after Kawasaki disease"

**PMC4209173:**

- "**therapeutic monitoring** through the use of the International Normalised Ratio"
- "wide inter-individual variation in the maintenance dose required (0.5 mg/day to 20 mg/day)"

**PMC6714673:**
- "VKORC1 and CYP2C9 polymorphisms are the major predicting factors of **warfarin dose requirement**"
- "vitamin K–rich diet can prevent the action of warfarin...and may need high warfarin maintenance dose"

**Key phenotype categories found across all 31 VKORC1-positive papers:**
| Phenotype | Evidence |
|---|---|
| Warfarin/VKA dose requirement | Core phenotype in virtually all papers; quantitative dose (mg/week) |
| Dose variability | Consistently described; VKORC1+CYP2C9 explain 30-55% of variance |
| Therapeutic range / INR control | Time in therapeutic range (TTR) mentioned; INR targets specified |
| Bleeding risk | Referenced as key adverse outcome of over-anticoagulation |
| Thromboembolism | Referenced as indication for therapy and outcome of under-anticoagulation |
| Heart valve replacement | Frequent indication (mechanical valve, mitral/aortic valve) |
| Atrial fibrillation | Appears as an indication in several papers |
| Time to stable dose | Mentioned as clinical outcome in PMC2515139 |
| Over-anticoagulation | Referenced in citations (Schalekamp acenocoumarol paper title) |

---

**Summary:**

1. All 10 papers listed in Result 1 are confirmed NOT relevant to rs9923231 — they cover CYP2C9/phenytoin pharmacogenetics (PMC2014334), breast cancer survival (PMC2745926), metformin/OCT3 transport (PMC2976715), thiopurine/leukemia (PMC2993460), MTHFR/ALL survival (PMC3505921), OPRM1/alcohol in adolescents (PMC3691683), CHRNA5/nicotine (PMC3778124), HIV/atazanavir stones (PMC4567270), ALDH1B1/alcohol dependence (PMC5464525), and OPRM1/suicidal ideation (PMC6385304).

2. The 31 VKORC1-positive papers cover warfarin and VKA dosing pharmacogenetics. Beyond warfarin, the key non-warfarin drugs mentioned in substantive body text are: **fluindione** (actually studied in PMC3384479, with VKORC1 directly linked to its dose), **acenocoumarol/Acitrom** (studied in PMC3125052; cited in references elsewhere), and **phenprocoumon** (referenced in citations only). The primary phenotypes documented are warfarin/VKA dose requirement, INR control/therapeutic range, bleeding, thromboembolism, and cardiac indications (heart valve replacement, atrial fibrillation).

results.content:
1→# Warfarin Dose Model for the Prediction of Stable Maintenance Dose in Indian Patients
     2→
     3→## Metadata
     4→**Authors:** Tejasvita Gaikwad, Kanjaksha Ghosh, Peter Avery, Farhad Kamali, Shrimati Shetty
     5→**Journal:** Clinical and Applied Thrombosis/Hemostasis
     6→**Date:** 2017 Jan 4
     7→**DOI:** [10.1177/1076029616683046](https://doi.org/10.1177/1076029616683046)
     8→**PMID:** 28049362
     9→**PMCID:** PMC6714673
    10→**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6714673/
    11→**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6714673/pdf/10.1177_1076029616683046.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6714673/pdf/10.1177_1076029616683046.pdf)
    12→
    13→## Abstract
    14→
    15→The main aim of this study was to screen various genetic and nongenetic factors that are known to alter warfarin response and to generate a model to predict stable warfarin maintenance dose for Indian patients. The study comprised of 300 warfarin-treated patients. Followed by extensive literature review, 10 single-nucleotide polymorphisms, that is, VKORC1-1639 G>A (rs9923231), CYP2C9*2 (rs1799853), CYP2C9*3 (rs1057910), FVII R353Q (rs6046), GGCX 12970 C>G (rs11676382), CALU c.*4A>G (rs1043550), EPHX1 c.337T>C (rs1051740), GGCX: c.214+597G>A (rs12714145), GGCX: 8016G>A (rs699664), and CYP4F2 V433M (rs2108622), and 5 nongenetic factors, that is, age, gender, smoking, alcoholism, and diet, were selected to find their association with warfarin response. The univariate analysis was carried out for 15 variables (10 genetic and 5 nongenetic). Five variables, that is, VKORC1-1639 G>A, CYP2C9*2, CYP2C9*3, age, and diet, were found to be significantly associated with warfarin response in univariate analysis. These 5 variables were entered in stepwise and multiple regression analysis to generate a prediction model for stable warfarin maintenance dose. The generated model scored R 2 of .67, which indicates that this model can explain 67% of warfarin dose variability. The generated model will help in prescribing more accurate warfarin maintenance dosing in Indian patients and will also help in minimizing warfarin-induced adverse drug reactions and a better quality of life in these patients.
    16→
    17→Keywords: warfarin, dosing algorithm, multiple regression analysis, India
    18→
    19→## Introduction
    20→
    21→The goal of pharmacogenetics is to predict patient’s response to a specific drug, based on the genetic variations, in order to deliver the best possible medical treatment. Prediction of the drug response of an individual can increase the success of therapies and reduce the incidence of adverse side effects. Till date, numerous single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) are identified in genes coding for liver enzymes responsible for drug deposition and pharmacokinetics. Warfarin (coumarin) therapy is one of the many clinical situations in which knowledge of pharmacogenomics can deliver safe personalized dosing.
    22→
    23→Several evidences indicate that major genetic variables responsible for individual pharmacological response to warfarin are polymorphisms in 2 genes, namely, vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1 (*VKORC1*) and cytochrome P450-2C9 (*CYP2C9*).^1–5^ Genetic variations in cytochrome P450-4F2 (*CYP4F2*), γ-glutamyl carboxylase (*GGCX*), epoxide hydrolase 1 (*EPHX1*), calumenin (*Calu*), factor IX (*F9*), and factor VII (*F7*) gene also have shown subsidiary association with warfarin response.^6–12^ Other nongenetic factors including ethnicity, age, body mass index, weight, and gender are also known to have a minor role in warfarin sensitivity.^13–19^ Based on pharmacogenetics, clinical, and demographic factors, population-based warfarin pharmacogenomic algorithms have been generated for various ethnic groups.^20–22^
    24→
    25→In this study, we aim to generate a prediction model for stable warfarin maintenance dose in Indian patients by comprehensive analysis of genetic and nongenetic factors using multiple linear regression model. The outcome of such study will help in prescribing more accurate warfarin dosing and will improve management of this challenging therapy by minimizing warfarin-induced adverse drug reactions in Indian patients.
    26→
    27→## Materials and Methods
    28→
    29→This study comprised of 300 patients who were prescribed warfarin for various clinical conditions. Patients were recruited from King Edward Memorial Hospital, Mumbai, BYL Nair Charitable Hospital, Mumbai, as well as clinics located in Mumbai and other parts of India. The study was approved by the institutional ethics committee review board for Research on Human Subjects of National Institute of Immunohaematology (ICMR). Patients on any other medication known to interact with warfarin and patients with abnormal renal or liver function test were excluded from the study. A clinical pro forma was designed to include all the relevant patient information such as international normalized ratio (INR) values, warfarin daily dose, changes in warfarin dose, gender, body weight, height, smoking, diet, race, bleeding complications, new thrombotic episodes, surgical procedures, or any other major health complications. Warfarin initiation and maintenance dose adjustment was blinded to genotype results. Initially, INR was tested every 2 to 4 days. After 2 consecutive therapeutic INR, testing interval was increased to 2 weeks. After 4 consecutive therapeutic INR, this was increased to 4 weeks and continued with the same monitoring interval.
    30→
    31→The minimum follow-up period was 6 months and the maximum period was 15 months. None of the patients were on any vitamin K supplements.
    32→
    33→### Operational Definitions
    34→
    35→### Selected Variables
    36→
    37→Followed by extensive literature review, 10 SNPs, that is, *VKORC1*-1639 G>A (rs9923231), *CYP2C9**2 (rs1799853), *CYP2C9**3 (rs1057910), *FVII* R353Q (rs6046), *GGCX* 12970 C>G (rs11676382), *CALU* c.*4A>G (rs1043550), *EPHX1* c.337T>C (rs1051740), *GGCX*: c.214+597G>A (rs12714145), *GGCX*: 8016G>A (rs699664), and *CYP4F2* V433M (rs2108622), and 5 nongenetic factors, that is, age, gender, smoking, alcoholism, and diet, were selected to see their association with warfarin dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation.
    38→
    39→### Blood Collection and Processing
    40→
    41→After obtaining informed consent, 10 mL of venous blood sample was collected in 3.13% sodium citrate (1:9 anticoagulant to blood) from each patient. The samples were spun at 4000 rpm for 15 minutes at 4°C. The platelet poor plasma was used for Prothrombin time-International normalized ratio (PT-INR) test. Usually, the test is performed within 1 hour of collection of blood. The cell pellet was preserved at −20°C for DNA analysis. DNA was extracted by standard phenol–chloroform method.
    42→
    43→### Genotyping
    44→
    45→The polymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplification was done using oligonucleotide primers (Sigma Chemicals, Bangalore, India) in a 96-well thermal cycler (Applied Biosystems, Carlsbad, California). Genotyping for 10 SNPs was carried out by restriction fragment length polymorphism (RFLP), allele-specific PCR, and direct sequencing method.
    46→
    47→#### Restriction fragment length polymorphism
    48→
    49→*VKORC1-1639* G>A (rs9923231), *CYP2C9**2 (rs1799853), *CYP2C9**3 (rs1057910), and *FVII* R353Q (rs6046) polymorphisms were genotyped using RFLP technique. Briefly, the protocol for DNA amplification was as follows: the PCR mix consisted of a final volume of 25 µL, containing 1.25 µL of 10 pmol each primers, 0.2 µL 25 mM deoxynucleotides triphosphate, 1.5 µL 25 mM MgCl_2_, 2.5 µL 10× complete buffer (Bioron, Ludwigshafen, Germany), 0.15 µL DNA Free Sensitive (DFS) Taq DNA polymerase (Bioron, Ludwigshafen, Germany), and 2 µL DNA sample. Ten microliters of amplified product was used for RFLP analysis using 5 units of *Hpa II*, *Ava II*, *Nsi I*, and *Msp I* restriction enzyme (New England Biolabs, Hertfordshire, United Kingdom) for *VKORC1*-1639 G/A, *CYP2C9*2*, *CYP2C9**3, and *FVII* R353Q genotypes, respectively. The PCR program and the primer sequences for genotyping are given in Table S1 (Online Resource 1).
    50→
    51→#### Allele-specific PCR method
    52→
    53→Following polymorphisms were genotyped by allele-specific PCR standardized in the laboratory: *CALU* c.*4A>G (rs1043550), *CYP4F2* V433M (rs2108622), *EPHX1* c.337T>C (rs1051740), *GGCX* c.214+597G>A (rs12714145), and *GGCX* 8016G>A (rs699664). Two primers were designed, one specific for the wild type and the other for variant, to specifically detect desired genotypes. The PCR conditions were standardized with known wild-type, heterozygous, and homozygous samples. The accuracy of standardized allele-specific PCR method was validated with direct sequencing method, and the data were in agreement.
    54→
    55→#### Direct sequencing
    56→
    57→*GGCX* 12970 C>G (rs11676382) polymorphism was genotyped by direct sequencing method. The DNA samples were amplified using oligonucleotide primers in a 96-well thermal cycler (Applied Biosystems Veriti, Carlsbad, California). Briefly, the protocol for DNA amplification was as follows: in a final volume of 25 µL, containing 1.25 µL of 10 pmol each primers, 0.2 µL 25 mM deoxynucleotides triphosphate, 0.5 µL 25 mM MgCl_2_, 2.5 µL 10× complete buffer (Bioron), 0.10 µL DFS Taq DNA polymerase (Bioron), and 2 µL DNA sample. Primer sequences and PCR program for the same are given in Table S2 (Online Resource 1). Amplified PCR products were purified using commercial kits (Invitrogen, Germany) followed by cycle sequencing using BigDye Terminator v3.1 cycle sequencing kit. Sequencing was performed on an ABI Prism Genetic analyzer.
    58→
    59→### Statistical Analysis
    60→
    61→Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium was estimated for all the analyzed polymorphisms using an Excel-based application (Microsoft Office 2007).
    62→
    63→Fisher exact test was used to calculate the *P* value. Data were shown to be significant if *P* value was ≤.05. Statistical analysis for Fisher exact was performed with the MedCalc software version 12.3.0 ([http://www.medcalc.org/calc/relative_risk.php](http://www.medcalc.org/calc/relative_risk.php)). In most cases, the mutant homozygotes and heterozygotes were combined and compared to the wild type. For CYP2C9, each mutant genotype was compared to the wild type. Warfarin dose was square rooted before analysis to approximately normalize the data and to equalize the variance. Univariate analysis was done using 1-way analysis of variance or *t* tests (2 groups) on the square root of dose.
    64→
    65→#### Multiple regression analysis
    66→
    67→Stepwise multiple regression analysis was carried out using statistical program “R” (version 3.1.3).
    68→
    69→## Results
    70→
    71→A total of 300 patients fitting into the inclusion criteria were screened for 15 variables. The demographic and clinical data are shown in [Table 1](#table1-1076029616683046).
    72→
    73→### Table 1.
    74→
    75→| Total number of patients: 300 |  |
    76→| --- | --- |
    77→| Male, n (%) | 177 (59) |
    78→| Female, n (%) | 123 (41) |
    79→| Age, median (range), years | 37 (19-80) |
    80→| BMI, median (range), kg/m2 | 22.10 (17.12-35.42) |
    81→| Duration of anticoagulant treatment, median (range), months | 6 (6-24) |
    82→| Stable average daily warfarin dose, mean (standard deviation), mg | 5.05 (±1.84) |
    83→| Indication for anticoagulation with warfarin, n (%) |  |
    84→| Atrial fibrillation | 8 (2.7) |
    85→| Cerebral venous thrombosis | 111 (37.0) |
    86→| Lower limb DVT | 123 (41) |
    87→| Upper limb DVT | 10 (3.3) |
    88→| Heart valve replacement | 37 (12.4) |
    89→| Young stroke | 7 (2.3) |
    90→| Others | 4 (1.3) |
    91→
    92→Table 1 Caption: Demographic and Clinical Data.
    93→
    94→Fifty-nine percent were males and age range was from 19 to 80 years. The mean daily stable warfarin dose was 5.05 (±1.84 mg). Cerebral venous thrombosis and lower limb deep vein thrombosis were the commonest types of thrombosis.
    95→
    96→### Univariate Analysis
    97→
    98→Univariate analysis was carried out to evaluate the association of genetic and nongenetic variables with warfarin response.
    99→
   100→#### Genetic factors
   101→
   102→The genotype frequencies of all the polymorphisms and their effect on warfarin dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation are shown in [Table 2](#table2-1076029616683046). Among the 10 polymorphisms analyzed, *VKORC1*-1639 G>A polymorphism showed significant association with reduced dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation (*P* < .001). The “A” allele carriers were found to require low warfarin dose and were posed with a higher risk of overanticoagulation. Among the 2 *CYP2C9* variants, *CYP2C9**3 variant allele carrier patients showed statistically significant association with lower dose requirement as well as risk of overanticoagulation (*P* < .001), while *CYP2C9**2 allele carrier patients showed relatively weak association (*P* < .05) as compared to *CYP2C9**3, but it was statistically significant. *CALU*c.*4A>G (rs1043550), *EPHX1* c.337T>C (rs1051740), *CYP4F2* V433 M (rs2108622), *GGCX*104+597 G>A (rs12714145), *GGCX* 8016G>A (rs699664), and *Factor VII* R353Q (rs6046) polymorphisms showed nonsignificant association with dose requirement and also no statistically significant association was found with risk of overanticoagulation. *GGCX* 12970 C>G (rs11676382) variant allele was found to be absent in the present series.

   137→
   138→#### Nongenetic factors
   139→
   140→Five nongenetic factors, that is age, gender, smoking, alcoholism, and diet were selected to study their association with warfarin dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation ([Table 3](#table3-1076029616683046)). Among the nongenetic factors, age was found to have significant association with risk of overanticoagulation (<60 years vs ≥60 years, *P* < .001). When the 2 age groups were compared, there was no significant difference in warfarin dose. However, it was significant across all age groups (*P* = .013). With increasing age, the requirement of warfarin dose reduced but the risk of overanticoagulation significantly increased in elderly patients. Gender and smoking were neither found significantly associated with overanticoagulation nor with dose requirement. Although, in the current study, alcoholism has not shown any association with warfarin dose requirement or risk of overanticoagulation, yet the effect of alcoholism on warfarin is unclear in this study, as many chronic alcoholic patients with liver dysfunction and abnormal liver function were excluded from the present study. Vegetarian or occasionally nonvegetarian patients were found to require significantly lower warfarin dose as compared to nonvegetarian patients (*P* = .013). However, no significant association was found with the risk of overanticoagulation.

   156→
   157→### Multiple Linear Regression Analysis
   158→
   159→Multiple regression analysis was carried out to generate a model to predict stable warfarin maintenance dose. All possible explanatory variables were considered, but only *VKORC1*-1639 G>A, *CYP2C9**2, *CYP2C9**3, age, and diet were found significant in a stepwise multiple linear regression model ([Table 4](#table4-1076029616683046)). Based on the multiple regression analysis, the following equation was generated to predict daily stable warfarin maintenance dose in Indian patients:

   180→*R* ^2^ of the multiple regression model was scored to .67, indicating that the prediction model could explain approximately 67% of dose variability. The maximum dose variability was explained by *CYP2C9 (*2 and *3)* and *VKORC1* genotypes, that is, 42% and 23%, respectively, followed by age and diet ([Table 5](#table5-1076029616683046)).

   193→
   194→## Discussion
   195→
   196→The pharmacogenetic data have been helping the clinicians in improving the patient care by way of reduction in adverse drug reactions or by decreasing the cost of treatment by minimizing hospital admissions. The aim of our study was to develop a model for prediction of stable warfarin maintenance dose in Indian patients. In the present study, of 15 analyzed markers, only 5 markers, that is, *CYP2C9**2, *CYP2C9**3, *VKORC1*-1639 G>A, age, and diet, were found to be significantly associated with warfarin response in univariate analysis.
   197→
   198→Many studies have reported that *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* polymorphisms are the major predicting factors of warfarin dose requirement, but the present study shows that integrating patient’s diet and age can marginally improve the power of dose prediction. It is reported that a vitamin K–rich diet can prevent the action of warfarin (vitamin K antagonist) and may need high warfarin maintenance dose,^23,24,25^ whereas Ford et al in a review of literature concluded that vitamin K supplementation may reduce variation in drug response caused by dietary intake.^26^ Few other studies have suggested that patients on warfarin need to maintain consistent dietary vitamin K intake to achieve more stable anticoagulation response.^23,27^ In the present study, nonvegetarian patients were found to require higher warfarin dose than the vegetarian patients.
   199→
   200→Reports have shown that warfarin dose is inversely proportional to age and also elderly patients (>75 years) are at more risk for bleeding complications (*P* < .05).^18,28–30^ In the present study, significant association of age with decreased warfarin dose requirement was observed and a linear trend across all ages was seen (*P* < .05). The risk of warfarin-induced bleeding was also significant when compared between the 2 age groups (ie, <60 years vs ≥60 years, *P* < .001).
   201→
   202→The generated prediction model scored *R* ^2^ to .67 indicates that this prediction model could explain nearly 67% dose variability. The prediction power of this study is quite good as compared to other reported prediction models in Indian patients.^31–34^ However, the remaining 33% dose variability is still unsolved. It might be due to the genetic and/or nongenetic factors that are yet to be identified. More well-designed and large-scale studies are needed to uncover these unknown factors affecting warfarin response. Although this is a comprehensive analysis of most of the genetic and nongenetic risk factors associated with warfarin sensitivity, yet there is a lack of systematic data on comedications and other comorbidities associated with warfarin response. For instance, although alcohol intake has been considered as one of the factors in the present analysis, chronic alcoholics with liver dysfunction had to be excluded from the present study. Although the generated model can predict warfarin dose modestly and not the exact dosing, it can still help in dividing patients into low-, intermediate-, and high-dose requirement groups, which will help in prescribing more appropriate stable warfarin maintenance dosage than the conventional dosing and will also help in minimizing warfarin-induced adverse drug reactions and a better quality of life in patients who are treated with warfarin.

[metadata] {"type": "text", "file": {"filePath": "/app/papers/PMC6714673.md", "content": "# Warfarin Dose Model for the Prediction of Stable Maintenance Dose in Indian Patients\n\n## Metadata\n**Authors:** Tejasvita Gaikwad, Kanjaksha Ghosh, Peter Avery, Farhad Kamali, Shrimati Shetty\n**Journal:** Clinical and Applied Thrombosis/Hemostasis\n**Date:** 2017 Jan 4\n**DOI:** [10.1177/1076029616683046](https://doi.org/10.1177/1076029616683046)\n**PMID:** 28049362\n**PMCID:** PMC6714673\n**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6714673/\n**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6714673/pdf/10.1177_1076029616683046.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6714673/pdf/10.1177_1076029616683046.pdf)\n\n## Abstract\n\nThe main aim of this study was to screen various genetic and nongenetic factors that are known to alter warfarin response and to generate a model to predict stable warfarin maintenance dose for Indian patients. The study comprised of 300 warfarin-treated patients. Followed by extensive literature review, 10 single-nucleotide polymorphisms, that is, VKORC1-1639 G>A (rs9923231), CYP2C9*2 (rs1799853), CYP2C9*3 (rs1057910), FVII R353Q (rs6046), GGCX 12970 C>G (rs11676382), CALU c.*4A>G (rs1043550), EPHX1 c.337T>C (rs1051740), GGCX: c.214+597G>A (rs12714145), GGCX: 8016G>A (rs699664), and CYP4F2 V433M (rs2108622), and 5 nongenetic factors, that is, age, gender, smoking, alcoholism, and diet, were selected to find their association with warfarin response. The univariate analysis was carried out for 15 variables (10 genetic and 5 nongenetic). Five variables, that is, VKORC1-1639 G>A, CYP2C9*2, CYP2C9*3, age, and diet, were found to be significantly associated with warfarin response in univariate analysis. These 5 variables were entered in stepwise and multiple regression analysis to generate a prediction model for stable warfarin maintenance dose. The generated model scored R 2 of .67, which indicates that this model can explain 67% of warfarin dose variability. The generated model will help in prescribing more accurate warfarin maintenance dosing in Indian patients and will also help in minimizing warfarin-induced adverse drug reactions and a better quality of life in these patients.\n\nKeywords: warfarin, dosing algorithm, multiple regression analysis, India\n\n## Introduction\n\nThe goal of pharmacogenetics is to predict patient’s response to a specific drug, based on the genetic variations, in order to deliver the best possible medical treatment. Prediction of the drug response of an individual can increase the success of therapies and reduce the incidence of adverse side effects. Till date, numerous single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) are identified in genes coding for liver enzymes responsible for drug deposition and pharmacokinetics. Warfarin (coumarin) therapy is one of the many clinical situations in which knowledge of pharmacogenomics can deliver safe personalized dosing.\n\nSeveral evidences indicate that major genetic variables responsible for individual pharmacological response to warfarin are polymorphisms in 2 genes, namely, vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1 (*VKORC1*) and cytochrome P450-2C9 (*CYP2C9*).^1–5^ Genetic variations in cytochrome P450-4F2 (*CYP4F2*), γ-glutamyl carboxylase (*GGCX*), epoxide hydrolase 1 (*EPHX1*), calumenin (*Calu*), factor IX (*F9*), and factor VII (*F7*) gene also have shown subsidiary association with warfarin response.^6–12^ Other nongenetic factors including ethnicity, age, body mass index, weight, and gender are also known to have a minor role in warfarin sensitivity.^13–19^ Based on pharmacogenetics, clinical, and demographic factors, population-based warfarin pharmacogenomic algorithms have been generated for various ethnic groups.^20–22^\n\nIn this study, we aim to generate a prediction model for stable warfarin maintenance dose in Indian patients by comprehensive analysis of genetic and nongenetic factors using multiple linear regression model. The outcome of such study will help in prescribing more accurate warfarin dosing and will improve management of this challenging therapy by minimizing warfarin-induced adverse drug reactions in Indian patients.\n\n## Materials and Methods\n\nThis study comprised of 300 patients who were prescribed warfarin for various clinical conditions. Patients were recruited from King Edward Memorial Hospital, Mumbai, BYL Nair Charitable Hospital, Mumbai, as well as clinics located in Mumbai and other parts of India. The study was approved by the institutional ethics committee review board for Research on Human Subjects of National Institute of Immunohaematology (ICMR). Patients on any other medication known to interact with warfarin and patients with abnormal renal or liver function test were excluded from the study. A clinical pro forma was designed to include all the relevant patient information such as international normalized ratio (INR) values, warfarin daily dose, changes in warfarin dose, gender, body weight, height, smoking, diet, race, bleeding complications, new thrombotic episodes, surgical procedures, or any other major health complications. Warfarin initiation and maintenance dose adjustment was blinded to genotype results. Initially, INR was tested every 2 to 4 days. After 2 consecutive therapeutic INR, testing interval was increased to 2 weeks. After 4 consecutive therapeutic INR, this was increased to 4 weeks and continued with the same monitoring interval.\n\nThe minimum follow-up period was 6 months and the maximum period was 15 months. None of the patients were on any vitamin K supplements.\n\n### Operational Definitions\n\n### Selected Variables\n\nFollowed by extensive literature review, 10 SNPs, that is, *VKORC1*-1639 G>A (rs9923231), *CYP2C9**2 (rs1799853), *CYP2C9**3 (rs1057910), *FVII* R353Q (rs6046), *GGCX* 12970 C>G (rs11676382), *CALU* c.*4A>G (rs1043550), *EPHX1* c.337T>C (rs1051740), *GGCX*: c.214+597G>A (rs12714145), *GGCX*: 8016G>A (rs699664), and *CYP4F2* V433M (rs2108622), and 5 nongenetic factors, that is, age, gender, smoking, alcoholism, and diet, were selected to see their association with warfarin dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation.\n\n### Blood Collection and Processing\n\nAfter obtaining informed consent, 10 mL of venous blood sample was collected in 3.13% sodium citrate (1:9 anticoagulant to blood) from each patient. The samples were spun at 4000 rpm for 15 minutes at 4°C. The platelet poor plasma was used for Prothrombin time-International normalized ratio (PT-INR) test. Usually, the test is performed within 1 hour of collection of blood. The cell pellet was preserved at −20°C for DNA analysis. DNA was extracted by standard phenol–chloroform method.\n\n### Genotyping\n\nThe polymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplification was done using oligonucleotide primers (Sigma Chemicals, Bangalore, India) in a 96-well thermal cycler (Applied Biosystems, Carlsbad, California). Genotyping for 10 SNPs was carried out by restriction fragment length polymorphism (RFLP), allele-specific PCR, and direct sequencing method.\n\n#### Restriction fragment length polymorphism\n\n*VKORC1-1639* G>A (rs9923231), *CYP2C9**2 (rs1799853), *CYP2C9**3 (rs1057910), and *FVII* R353Q (rs6046) polymorphisms were genotyped using RFLP technique. Briefly, the protocol for DNA amplification was as follows: the PCR mix consisted of a final volume of 25 µL, containing 1.25 µL of 10 pmol each primers, 0.2 µL 25 mM deoxynucleotides triphosphate, 1.5 µL 25 mM MgCl_2_, 2.5 µL 10× complete buffer (Bioron, Ludwigshafen, Germany), 0.15 µL DNA Free Sensitive (DFS) Taq DNA polymerase (Bioron, Ludwigshafen, Germany), and 2 µL DNA sample. Ten microliters of amplified product was used for RFLP analysis using 5 units of *Hpa II*, *Ava II*, *Nsi I*, and *Msp I* restriction enzyme (New England Biolabs, Hertfordshire, United Kingdom) for *VKORC1*-1639 G/A, *CYP2C9*2*, *CYP2C9**3, and *FVII* R353Q genotypes, respectively. The PCR program and the primer sequences for genotyping are given in Table S1 (Online Resource 1).\n\n#### Allele-specific PCR method\n\nFollowing polymorphisms were genotyped by allele-specific PCR standardized in the laboratory: *CALU* c.*4A>G (rs1043550), *CYP4F2* V433M (rs2108622), *EPHX1* c.337T>C (rs1051740), *GGCX* c.214+597G>A (rs12714145), and *GGCX* 8016G>A (rs699664). Two primers were designed, one specific for the wild type and the other for variant, to specifically detect desired genotypes. The PCR conditions were standardized with known wild-type, heterozygous, and homozygous samples. The accuracy of standardized allele-specific PCR method was validated with direct sequencing method, and the data were in agreement.\n\n#### Direct sequencing\n\n*GGCX* 12970 C>G (rs11676382) polymorphism was genotyped by direct sequencing method. The DNA samples were amplified using oligonucleotide primers in a 96-well thermal cycler (Applied Biosystems Veriti, Carlsbad, California). Briefly, the protocol for DNA amplification was as follows: in a final volume of 25 µL, containing 1.25 µL of 10 pmol each primers, 0.2 µL 25 mM deoxynucleotides triphosphate, 0.5 µL 25 mM MgCl_2_, 2.5 µL 10× complete buffer (Bioron), 0.10 µL DFS Taq DNA polymerase (Bioron), and 2 µL DNA sample. Primer sequences and PCR program for the same are given in Table S2 (Online Resource 1). Amplified PCR products were purified using commercial kits (Invitrogen, Germany) followed by cycle sequencing using BigDye Terminator v3.1 cycle sequencing kit. Sequencing was performed on an ABI Prism Genetic analyzer.\n\n### Statistical Analysis\n\nHardy-Weinberg equilibrium was estimated for all the analyzed polymorphisms using an Excel-based application (Microsoft Office 2007).\n\nFisher exact test was used to calculate the *P* value. Data were shown to be significant if *P* value was ≤.05. Statistical analysis for Fisher exact was performed with the MedCalc software version 12.3.0 ([http://www.medcalc.org/calc/relative_risk.php](http://www.medcalc.org/calc/relative_risk.php)). In most cases, the mutant homozygotes and heterozygotes were combined and compared to the wild type. For CYP2C9, each mutant genotype was compared to the wild type. Warfarin dose was square rooted before analysis to approximately normalize the data and to equalize the variance. Univariate analysis was done using 1-way analysis of variance or *t* tests (2 groups) on the square root of dose.\n\n#### Multiple regression analysis\n\nStepwise multiple regression analysis was carried out using statistical program “R” (version 3.1.3).\n\n## Results\n\nA total of 300 patients fitting into the inclusion criteria were screened for 15 variables. The demographic and clinical data are shown in [Table 1](#table1-1076029616683046).\n\n### Table 1.\n\n| Total number of patients: 300 |  |\n| --- | --- |\n| Male, n (%) | 177 (59) |\n| Female, n (%) | 123 (41) |\n| Age, median (range), years | 37 (19-80) |\n| BMI, median (range), kg/m2 | 22.10 (17.12-35.42) |\n| Duration of anticoagulant treatment, median (range), months | 6 (6-24) |\n| Stable average daily warfarin dose, mean (standard deviation), mg | 5.05 (±1.84) |\n| Indication for anticoagulation with warfarin, n (%) |  |\n| Atrial fibrillation | 8 (2.7) |\n| Cerebral venous thrombosis | 111 (37.0) |\n| Lower limb DVT | 123 (41) |\n| Upper limb DVT | 10 (3.3) |\n| Heart valve replacement | 37 (12.4) |\n| Young stroke | 7 (2.3) |\n| Others | 4 (1.3) |\n\nTable 1 Caption: Demographic and Clinical Data.\n\nFifty-nine percent were males and age range was from 19 to 80 years. The mean daily stable warfarin dose was 5.05 (±1.84 mg). Cerebral venous thrombosis and lower limb deep vein thrombosis were the commonest types of thrombosis.\n\n### Univariate Analysis\n\nUnivariate analysis was carried out to evaluate the association of genetic and nongenetic variables with warfarin response.\n\n#### Genetic factors\n\nThe genotype frequencies of all the polymorphisms and their effect on warfarin dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation are shown in [Table 2](#table2-1076029616683046). Among the 10 polymorphisms analyzed, *VKORC1*-1639 G>A polymorphism showed significant association with reduced dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation (*P* < .001). The “A” allele carriers were found to require low warfarin dose and were posed with a higher risk of overanticoagulation. Among the 2 *CYP2C9* variants, *CYP2C9**3 variant allele carrier patients showed statistically significant association with lower dose requirement as well as risk of overanticoagulation (*P* < .001), while *CYP2C9**2 allele carrier patients showed relatively weak association (*P* < .05) as compared to *CYP2C9**3, but it was statistically significant. *CALU*c.*4A>G (rs1043550), *EPHX1* c.337T>C (rs1051740), *CYP4F2* V433 M (rs2108622), *GGCX*104+597 G>A (rs12714145), *GGCX* 8016G>A (rs699664), and *Factor VII* R353Q (rs6046) polymorphisms showed nonsignificant association with dose requirement and also no statistically significant association was found with risk of overanticoagulation. *GGCX* 12970 C>G (rs11676382) variant allele was found to be absent in the present series.\n\n| Polymorphism | Genotype | N = 300, n (%) | Mean Daily Warfarin Dose (mg/d) | P Values for Mean Daily Warfarin Dosea | Patients Faced Overanticoagulation (INR > 4), n (%) | P Values for Overanticoagulation (INR > 4) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| VKORC1-1639G>A (rs9923231) | GG | 223 (74) | 5.55 | - | 41 (44) | - |\n| GA | 178 (59) | 5.03 | .505 | 75 (42) | .9839 |  |\n| AA | 29 (10) | 5.38 | 16 (55) |  |  |  |\n| FVII R353Q 13407G>A (rs6046) | GG | 167 (56) | 5.15 | - | 66 (39) | - |\n| GA | 122 (40) | 4.86 | .213 | 64 (52) | .0790 |  |\n| AA | 11 (4) | 5.63 | 2 (18) |  |  |  |\n\nTable Caption: Effect of Genetic Polymorphisms on Warfarin Dose Requirement and Risk of Overanticoagulation.\n\n#### Nongenetic factors\n\nFive nongenetic factors, that is age, gender, smoking, alcoholism, and diet were selected to study their association with warfarin dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation ([Table 3](#table3-1076029616683046)). Among the nongenetic factors, age was found to have significant association with risk of overanticoagulation (<60 years vs ≥60 years, *P* < .001). When the 2 age groups were compared, there was no significant difference in warfarin dose. However, it was significant across all age groups (*P* = .013). With increasing age, the requirement of warfarin dose reduced but the risk of overanticoagulation significantly increased in elderly patients. Gender and smoking were neither found significantly associated with overanticoagulation nor with dose requirement. Although, in the current study, alcoholism has not shown any association with warfarin dose requirement or risk of overanticoagulation, yet the effect of alcoholism on warfarin is unclear in this study, as many chronic alcoholic patients with liver dysfunction and abnormal liver function were excluded from the present study. Vegetarian or occasionally nonvegetarian patients were found to require significantly lower warfarin dose as compared to nonvegetarian patients (*P* = .013). However, no significant association was found with the risk of overanticoagulation.\n\n| Characteristic | Parameters | N = 300 (%) | Mean Daily Warfarin Dose (mg/d) | P Values for Mean Daily Warfarin Dosea | Patients Who Faced Overanticoagulation (INR > 4), n (%) | P Values for Overanticoagulation (INR > 4) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Age | <60 years | 279 (93) | 5.07 | - | 115 (41) | - |\n| ≥60 years | 21 (7) | 4.74 | .53 | 17 (81) | .001 |  |\n| Gender | Male | 177 (59) | 4.95 |   | 77 (43) |   |\n| Female | 123 (41) | 5.19 | .338 | 55 (43) | .8348 |  |\n| Smoking | No | 268 (89) | 5.05 | - | 121 (45) | - |\n| Yes | 32 (11) | 5.01 | .686 | 11 (34) | .2819 |  |\n| Chronic alcoholic | No | 260 (87) | 5.02 | - | 119 (46) | - |\n| Yes | 40 (13) | 5.24 | .888 | 13 (32) | .1497 |  |\n| Diet | Nonvegetarians | 214 (71) | 5.23 | - | 92 (43) | - |\n| Vegetarian or occasionally nonvegetarian | 86 (29) | 4.61 | .013 | 40 (47) | .5736 |  |\n\nTable Caption: Effect of Nongenetic Factors on Warfarin Dose Requirement and Risk of Overanticoagulation.\n\n### Multiple Linear Regression Analysis\n\nMultiple regression analysis was carried out to generate a model to predict stable warfarin maintenance dose. All possible explanatory variables were considered, but only *VKORC1*-1639 G>A, *CYP2C9**2, *CYP2C9**3, age, and diet were found significant in a stepwise multiple linear regression model ([Table 4](#table4-1076029616683046)). Based on the multiple regression analysis, the following equation was generated to predict daily stable warfarin maintenance dose in Indian patients:\n\n| Square root of dose(mg)=2.61−0.41(VKORCI−1639)−0.21(*1/*2)−0.58(*1/*3)−0.86(*2/*3)−0.86(*3/*3)−0.002(Age in years)−0.08(diet). |\n| --- |\n### Table 4.\n\n| Coefficients | Estimate | Standard Error | Pr (>\\|t\\|) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| (Intercept) | 2.609832 | 0.046119 | <2.0 × 10−16a |\n| VKORC1-1639 G>A | −0.406740 | 0.030869 | <2.0 × 10−16 a |\n| CYP2C9*1/*2 | −0.206816 | 0.068246 | 0.00266b |\n| CYP2C9*1/*3 | −0.579309 | 0.037153 | <2.0 × 10−16 a |\n| CYP2C9*2/*3 | −0.862982 | 0.126904 | 5.93 × 10−11 a |\n| CYP2C9*3/*3 | −0.864116 | 0.081463 | <2.0 × 10−16 a |\n| Age | −0.002576 | 0.001103 | 0.02023c |\n| Vegetarian | −0.082231 | 0.032153 | 0.01105c |\n\nTable 4 Caption: Multiple Linear Regression Analysis.\n\nVKORC1-1639: “0” for GG, “1” for GA, “2” for AA; CYP2C9 *1/*2, *1/*3, *2/*3, and *3/*3, “0” if variant genotype is absent, and “1” if variant genotype is present; “0” if patient is nonvegetarian and “1” if patient is vegetarian or occasionally nonvegetarian.\n\n*R* ^2^ of the multiple regression model was scored to .67, indicating that the prediction model could explain approximately 67% of dose variability. The maximum dose variability was explained by *CYP2C9 (*2 and *3)* and *VKORC1* genotypes, that is, 42% and 23%, respectively, followed by age and diet ([Table 5](#table5-1076029616683046)).\n\n### Table 5.\n\n| Predictor | Partial R 2 (%) | P Value |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| VKORC1-1639 G>A | 23 | .001 |\n| CYP2C9*2 and CYP2C9*3 genotype | 42 | .001 |\n| Age | 0.80 | .01 |\n| Diet | 0.70 | .05 |\n| Model R 2 | 67 | .001 |\n\nTable 5 Caption: Contribution of Factors in Multiple Linear Regression Model.\n\n## Discussion\n\nThe pharmacogenetic data have been helping the clinicians in improving the patient care by way of reduction in adverse drug reactions or by decreasing the cost of treatment by minimizing hospital admissions. The aim of our study was to develop a model for prediction of stable warfarin maintenance dose in Indian patients. In the present study, of 15 analyzed markers, only 5 markers, that is, *CYP2C9**2, *CYP2C9**3, *VKORC1*-1639 G>A, age, and diet, were found to be significantly associated with warfarin response in univariate analysis.\n\nMany studies have reported that *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* polymorphisms are the major predicting factors of warfarin dose requirement, but the present study shows that integrating patient’s diet and age can marginally improve the power of dose prediction. It is reported that a vitamin K–rich diet can prevent the action of warfarin (vitamin K antagonist) and may need high warfarin maintenance dose,^23,24,25^ whereas Ford et al in a review of literature concluded that vitamin K supplementation may reduce variation in drug response caused by dietary intake.^26^ Few other studies have suggested that patients on warfarin need to maintain consistent dietary vitamin K intake to achieve more stable anticoagulation response.^23,27^ In the present study, nonvegetarian patients were found to require higher warfarin dose than the vegetarian patients.\n\nReports have shown that warfarin dose is inversely proportional to age and also elderly patients (>75 years) are at more risk for bleeding complications (*P* < .05).^18,28–30^ In the present study, significant association of age with decreased warfarin dose requirement was observed and a linear trend across all ages was seen (*P* < .05). The risk of warfarin-induced bleeding was also significant when compared between the 2 age groups (ie, <60 years vs ≥60 years, *P* < .001).\n\nThe generated prediction model scored *R* ^2^ to .67 indicates that this prediction model could explain nearly 67% dose variability. The prediction power of this study is quite good as compared to other reported prediction models in Indian patients.^31–34^ However, the remaining 33% dose variability is still unsolved. It might be due to the genetic and/or nongenetic factors that are yet to be identified. More well-designed and large-scale studies are needed to uncover these unknown factors affecting warfarin response. Although this is a comprehensive analysis of most of the genetic and nongenetic risk factors associated with warfarin sensitivity, yet there is a lack of systematic data on comedications and other comorbidities associated with warfarin response. For instance, although alcohol intake has been considered as one of the factors in the present analysis, chronic alcoholics with liver dysfunction had to be excluded from the present study. Although the generated model can predict warfarin dose modestly and not the exact dosing, it can still help in dividing patients into low-, intermediate-, and high-dose requirement groups, which will help in prescribing more appropriate stable warfarin maintenance dosage than the conventional dosing and will also help in minimizing warfarin-induced adverse drug reactions and a better quality of life in patients who are treated with warfarin.\n\n## Supplementary Material\n\n## Acknowledgments\n\nThe authors are grateful to Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), Delhi, India, for providing Research Fellowship to one of the authors (TG; IRIS cell No. 2011–05540). # Warfarin Dose Model for the Prediction of Stable Maintenance Dose in Indian Patients

## Metadata
**Authors:** Tejasvita Gaikwad, Kanjaksha Ghosh, Peter Avery, Farhad Kamali, Shrimati Shetty
**Journal:** Clinical and Applied Thrombosis/Hemostasis
**Date:** 2017 Jan 4
**DOI:** [10.1177/1076029616683046](https://doi.org/10.1177/1076029616683046)
**PMID:** 28049362
**PMCID:** PMC6714673
**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6714673/
**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6714673/pdf/10.1177_1076029616683046.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6714673/pdf/10.1177_1076029616683046.pdf)

## Abstract

The main aim of this study was to screen various genetic and nongenetic factors that are known to alter warfarin response and to generate a model to predict stable warfarin maintenance dose for Indian patients. The study comprised of 300 warfarin-treated patients. Followed by extensive literature review, 10 single-nucleotide polymorphisms, that is, VKORC1-1639 G>A (rs9923231), CYP2C9*2 (rs1799853), CYP2C9*3 (rs1057910), FVII R353Q (rs6046), GGCX 12970 C>G (rs11676382), CALU c.*4A>G (rs1043550), EPHX1 c.337T>C (rs1051740), GGCX: c.214+597G>A (rs12714145), GGCX: 8016G>A (rs699664), and CYP4F2 V433M (rs2108622), and 5 nongenetic factors, that is, age, gender, smoking, alcoholism, and diet, were selected to find their association with warfarin response. The univariate analysis was carried out for 15 variables (10 genetic and 5 nongenetic). Five variables, that is, VKORC1-1639 G>A, CYP2C9*2, CYP2C9*3, age, and diet, were found to be significantly associated with warfarin response in univariate analysis. These 5 variables were entered in stepwise and multiple regression analysis to generate a prediction model for stable warfarin maintenance dose. The generated model scored R 2 of .67, which indicates that this model can explain 67% of warfarin dose variability. The generated model will help in prescribing more accurate warfarin maintenance dosing in Indian patients and will also help in minimizing warfarin-induced adverse drug reactions and a better quality of life in these patients.

Keywords: warfarin, dosing algorithm, multiple regression analysis, India

## Introduction

The goal of pharmacogenetics is to predict patient’s response to a specific drug, based on the genetic variations, in order to deliver the best possible medical treatment. Prediction of the drug response of an individual can increase the success of therapies and reduce the incidence of adverse side effects. Till date, numerous single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) are identified in genes coding for liver enzymes responsible for drug deposition and pharmacokinetics. Warfarin (coumarin) therapy is one of the many clinical situations in which knowledge of pharmacogenomics can deliver safe personalized dosing.

Several evidences indicate that major genetic variables responsible for individual pharmacological response to warfarin are polymorphisms in 2 genes, namely, vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1 (*VKORC1*) and cytochrome P450-2C9 (*CYP2C9*).^1–5^ Genetic variations in cytochrome P450-4F2 (*CYP4F2*), γ-glutamyl carboxylase (*GGCX*), epoxide hydrolase 1 (*EPHX1*), calumenin (*Calu*), factor IX (*F9*), and factor VII (*F7*) gene also have shown subsidiary association with warfarin response.^6–12^ Other nongenetic factors including ethnicity, age, body mass index, weight, and gender are also known to have a minor role in warfarin sensitivity.^13–19^ Based on pharmacogenetics, clinical, and demographic factors, population-based warfarin pharmacogenomic algorithms have been generated for various ethnic groups.^20–22^

In this study, we aim to generate a prediction model for stable warfarin maintenance dose in Indian patients by comprehensive analysis of genetic and nongenetic factors using multiple linear regression model. The outcome of such study will help in prescribing more accurate warfarin dosing and will improve management of this challenging therapy by minimizing warfarin-induced adverse drug reactions in Indian patients.

## Materials and Methods

This study comprised of 300 patients who were prescribed warfarin for various clinical conditions. Patients were recruited from King Edward Memorial Hospital, Mumbai, BYL Nair Charitable Hospital, Mumbai, as well as clinics located in Mumbai and other parts of India. The study was approved by the institutional ethics committee review board for Research on Human Subjects of National Institute of Immunohaematology (ICMR). Patients on any other medication known to interact with warfarin and patients with abnormal renal or liver function test were excluded from the study. A clinical pro forma was designed to include all the relevant patient information such as international normalized ratio (INR) values, warfarin daily dose, changes in warfarin dose, gender, body weight, height, smoking, diet, race, bleeding complications, new thrombotic episodes, surgical procedures, or any other major health complications. Warfarin initiation and maintenance dose adjustment was blinded to genotype results. Initially, INR was tested every 2 to 4 days. After 2 consecutive therapeutic INR, testing interval was increased to 2 weeks. After 4 consecutive therapeutic INR, this was increased to 4 weeks and continued with the same monitoring interval.

The minimum follow-up period was 6 months and the maximum period was 15 months. None of the patients were on any vitamin K supplements.

### Operational Definitions

### Selected Variables

Followed by extensive literature review, 10 SNPs, that is, *VKORC1*-1639 G>A (rs9923231), *CYP2C9**2 (rs1799853), *CYP2C9**3 (rs1057910), *FVII* R353Q (rs6046), *GGCX* 12970 C>G (rs11676382), *CALU* c.*4A>G (rs1043550), *EPHX1* c.337T>C (rs1051740), *GGCX*: c.214+597G>A (rs12714145), *GGCX*: 8016G>A (rs699664), and *CYP4F2* V433M (rs2108622), and 5 nongenetic factors, that is, age, gender, smoking, alcoholism, and diet, were selected to see their association with warfarin dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation.

### Blood Collection and Processing

After obtaining informed consent, 10 mL of venous blood sample was collected in 3.13% sodium citrate (1:9 anticoagulant to blood) from each patient. The samples were spun at 4000 rpm for 15 minutes at 4°C. The platelet poor plasma was used for Prothrombin time-International normalized ratio (PT-INR) test. Usually, the test is performed within 1 hour of collection of blood. The cell pellet was preserved at −20°C for DNA analysis. DNA was extracted by standard phenol–chloroform method.

### Genotyping

The polymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplification was done using oligonucleotide primers (Sigma Chemicals, Bangalore, India) in a 96-well thermal cycler (Applied Biosystems, Carlsbad, California). Genotyping for 10 SNPs was carried out by restriction fragment length polymorphism (RFLP), allele-specific PCR, and direct sequencing method.

#### Restriction fragment length polymorphism

*VKORC1-1639* G>A (rs9923231), *CYP2C9**2 (rs1799853), *CYP2C9**3 (rs1057910), and *FVII* R353Q (rs6046) polymorphisms were genotyped using RFLP technique. Briefly, the protocol for DNA amplification was as follows: the PCR mix consisted of a final volume of 25 µL, containing 1.25 µL of 10 pmol each primers, 0.2 µL 25 mM deoxynucleotides triphosphate, 1.5 µL 25 mM MgCl_2_, 2.5 µL 10× complete buffer (Bioron, Ludwigshafen, Germany), 0.15 µL DNA Free Sensitive (DFS) Taq DNA polymerase (Bioron, Ludwigshafen, Germany), and 2 µL DNA sample. Ten microliters of amplified product was used for RFLP analysis using 5 units of *Hpa II*, *Ava II*, *Nsi I*, and *Msp I* restriction enzyme (New England Biolabs, Hertfordshire, United Kingdom) for *VKORC1*-1639 G/A, *CYP2C9*2*, *CYP2C9**3, and *FVII* R353Q genotypes, respectively. The PCR program and the primer sequences for genotyping are given in Table S1 (Online Resource 1).

#### Allele-specific PCR method

Following polymorphisms were genotyped by allele-specific PCR standardized in the laboratory: *CALU* c.*4A>G (rs1043550), *CYP4F2* V433M (rs2108622), *EPHX1* c.337T>C (rs1051740), *GGCX* c.214+597G>A (rs12714145), and *GGCX* 8016G>A (rs699664). Two primers were designed, one specific for the wild type and the other for variant, to specifically detect desired genotypes. The PCR conditions were standardized with known wild-type, heterozygous, and homozygous samples. The accuracy of standardized allele-specific PCR method was validated with direct sequencing method, and the data were in agreement.

#### Direct sequencing

*GGCX* 12970 C>G (rs11676382) polymorphism was genotyped by direct sequencing method. The DNA samples were amplified using oligonucleotide primers in a 96-well thermal cycler (Applied Biosystems Veriti, Carlsbad, California). Briefly, the protocol for DNA amplification was as follows: in a final volume of 25 µL, containing 1.25 µL of 10 pmol each primers, 0.2 µL 25 mM deoxynucleotides triphosphate, 0.5 µL 25 mM MgCl_2_, 2.5 µL 10× complete buffer (Bioron), 0.10 µL DFS Taq DNA polymerase (Bioron), and 2 µL DNA sample. Primer sequences and PCR program for the same are given in Table S2 (Online Resource 1). Amplified PCR products were purified using commercial kits (Invitrogen, Germany) followed by cycle sequencing using BigDye Terminator v3.1 cycle sequencing kit. Sequencing was performed on an ABI Prism Genetic analyzer.

### Statistical Analysis

Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium was estimated for all the analyzed polymorphisms using an Excel-based application (Microsoft Office 2007).

Fisher exact test was used to calculate the *P* value. Data were shown to be significant if *P* value was ≤.05. Statistical analysis for Fisher exact was performed with the MedCalc software version 12.3.0 ([http://www.medcalc.org/calc/relative_risk.php](http://www.medcalc.org/calc/relative_risk.php)). In most cases, the mutant homozygotes and heterozygotes were combined and compared to the wild type. For CYP2C9, each mutant genotype was compared to the wild type. Warfarin dose was square rooted before analysis to approximately normalize the data and to equalize the variance. Univariate analysis was done using 1-way analysis of variance or *t* tests (2 groups) on the square root of dose.

#### Multiple regression analysis

Stepwise multiple regression analysis was carried out using statistical program “R” (version 3.1.3).

Fifty-nine percent were males and age range was from 19 to 80 years. The mean daily stable warfarin dose was 5.05 (±1.84 mg). Cerebral venous thrombosis and lower limb deep vein thrombosis were the commonest types of thrombosis.

### Univariate Analysis

Univariate analysis was carried out to evaluate the association of genetic and nongenetic variables with warfarin response.

#### Genetic factors

The genotype frequencies of all the polymorphisms and their effect on warfarin dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation are shown in [Table 2](#table2-1076029616683046). Among the 10 polymorphisms analyzed, *VKORC1*-1639 G>A polymorphism showed significant association with reduced dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation (*P* < .001). The “A” allele carriers were found to require low warfarin dose and were posed with a higher risk of overanticoagulation. Among the 2 *CYP2C9* variants, *CYP2C9**3 variant allele carrier patients showed statistically significant association with lower dose requirement as well as risk of overanticoagulation (*P* < .001), while *CYP2C9**2 allele carrier patients showed relatively weak association (*P* < .05) as compared to *CYP2C9**3, but it was statistically significant. *CALU*c.*4A>G (rs1043550), *EPHX1* c.337T>C (rs1051740), *CYP4F2* V433 M (rs2108622), *GGCX*104+597 G>A (rs12714145), *GGCX* 8016G>A (rs699664), and *Factor VII* R353Q (rs6046) polymorphisms showed nonsignificant association with dose requirement and also no statistically significant association was found with risk of overanticoagulation. *GGCX* 12970 C>G (rs11676382) variant allele was found to be absent in the present series.

#### Nongenetic factors

Five nongenetic factors, that is age, gender, smoking, alcoholism, and diet were selected to study their association with warfarin dose requirement and risk of overanticoagulation ([Table 3](#table3-1076029616683046)). Among the nongenetic factors, age was found to have significant association with risk of overanticoagulation (<60 years vs ≥60 years, *P* < .001). When the 2 age groups were compared, there was no significant difference in warfarin dose. However, it was significant across all age groups (*P* = .013). With increasing age, the requirement of warfarin dose reduced but the risk of overanticoagulation significantly increased in elderly patients. Gender and smoking were neither found significantly associated with overanticoagulation nor with dose requirement. Although, in the current study, alcoholism has not shown any association with warfarin dose requirement or risk of overanticoagulation, yet the effect of alcoholism on warfarin is unclear in this study, as many chronic alcoholic patients with liver dysfunction and abnormal liver function were excluded from the present study. Vegetarian or occasionally nonvegetarian patients were found to require significantly lower warfarin dose as compared to nonvegetarian patients (*P* = .013). However, no significant association was found with the risk of overanticoagulation.
